title: Will Even This Wish of the Tamil People Be Fulfilled?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. The Tamil section is a source-checked reading copy derived from the OCR and does not alter the canonical source file. Visual review restored the opening `உடன்பிறப்பே,`, omitted by OCR. Other repairs are limited to unambiguous broken word joins. The source refers first to Government Order No. 105 dated 24-1-1968 and later to the same order number as dated 24-1-1965; both source-printed dates are preserved rather than silently harmonised. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: All the newspapers have carried a report under the headline: “Tamil language subject compulsory in matriculation schools: Case seeks stay of government order, notice to Tamil Nadu government.” In other words, a person has filed a petition in the Madras High Court seeking a stay on implementing the law that makes Tamil a compulsory subject in matriculation schools. The High Court has directed that notice be issued to the Tamil Nadu government, requiring it to reply by 10 June.

When the DMK returned to office in 2006, the Governor’s Address read in the Legislative Assembly itself stated: “This government assures the House that it will quickly consult educationists, remove the obstacles involved, and issue an order requiring every boy and girl studying in school to learn Tamil as a compulsory subject from the coming academic year.”

Indeed, even earlier, the movement’s election manifesto had announced: “We will find an appropriate way for every student studying in the various kinds of schools operating in Tamil Nadu to learn Tamil as a language subject.” It was in order to do what we had promised that, immediately after assuming office, we included that election pledge in the Governor’s Address.

In paragraph 40 of the Budget presented later that year, we announced: “Following the election manifesto and in fulfilment of the assurance in the Governor’s Address, legislation has been enacted requiring every school student to learn our Tamil language, ending the shameful situation in which ‘Tamil alone is absent from the schools of Tamil Nadu.’ Under this law, Tamil will begin to be taught as a subject in Standard I in every school this very year. It will be extended progressively up to Standard X in the years ahead.”

What language policy has been followed in Tamil Nadu? What did the DMK do while in office? What has the present AIADMK government done for Tamil-language education? Through this letter, I wish to place before you several facts that answer these questions.
In the Governor’s Address delivered on behalf of the AIADMK government in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly on 9-3-2002, it was announced:
“It will be ensured that Tamil is taught as a subject in the curriculum of every school in the state.”

The AIADMK government took no action on this announcement for a year. On 21 April 2003, it issued a government order saying that “Scientific Tamil” would be taught in Standards I to V so that everyone in the schools could learn it.

The government order issued by Jayalalithaa’s government on 21-4-2003 quoted the very words of Government Order No. 105, issued on 24-1-1968 when Perarignar Anna was Chief Minister, and claimed that the same policy was being followed.

But under DMK rule, Government Order No. 354 dated 27-12-1999 amended the words “regional language or mother tongue” in Government Order No. 105 dated 24-1-1965 to read “Tamil or mother tongue.” This amendment issued by the DMK government in 1999 addressed the concern of certain Tamil scholars that the word “Tamil” itself did not appear in the government order establishing the two-language policy.

Yet in the government order Jayalalithaa issued on 21-4-2003, no attention was paid to the DMK government’s amendment inserting “Tamil” into the order on the two-language policy. Instead, her government proceeded as though the order issued in Tamil Nadu in 1968 remained unchanged.

During the DMK government, a letter sent to the Director of Elementary Education on 28-8-1997 stressed the necessity of mother-tongue education and directed that school administrations be advised of the extent to which children benefit by studying in their mother tongue at a young age.

Government Order No. 421 dated 27-11-1998 announced that Tamil should be introduced as the medium of instruction in all nursery and kindergarten schools. A fifty-percent concession in registration fees and similar charges was offered to those who implemented it. This was an action taken under DMK rule to encourage Tamil education.

Government Order No. 6 dated 13-1-1999 stated that recognition would be renewed only for schools that taught in Tamil two of the three subjects, mathematics, science and social science. It also ordered that textbooks be provided free to nursery and kindergarten schools using Tamil as the medium of instruction. This was another DMK-government measure to encourage Tamil education.

Under Government Order No. 117 dated 3-5-1999, the government appointed a four-member committee headed by Justice S. Mohan to examine the request from Tamil scholars that Tamil be introduced as the medium of instruction from nursery through higher secondary education. This too was an action taken for Tamil by the DMK government.

Several associations filed writ petitions in the Madras High Court against the government order issued on 13-1-1999. In its judgment of 7-6-1999, the High Court upheld the order but said it would not apply to students whose mother tongue was not Tamil. Consequently, on 27-1-2000 the government issued an amendment to its order of 13-1-1999, stating that the medium of instruction could be Tamil or the mother tongue.
On the basis of the recommendations of the Justice S. Mohan Committee, Government Order No. 324 was issued on 19-11-1999. It provided:
1. Orders concerning the government’s language policies would continue to be announced through government orders.
2. Tamil or the mother tongue would be the first language in every school, including matriculation schools, government schools, and recognised aided and unaided schools.
3. Tamil or the mother tongue would be the medium of instruction in matriculation and state government schools.
4. The transition to Tamil or the mother tongue as the medium of instruction was to be completed within three years, beginning in 2000-2001.

Under Government Order No. 355 dated 27-12-1999, Tamil or the mother tongue would be the medium of instruction from Standards I to V in matriculation schools. From Standard VI onward, the medium could be Tamil, the mother tongue or English.

Various associations filed writ petitions before the first bench of the High Court against Government Order No. 324, issued on 19-11-1999 on Justice Mohan’s recommendations. A full bench of the High Court allowed the petitions and quashed the order.
The Tamil Nadu government appealed the full-bench judgment before the Supreme Court, where the matter remains pending.

It was therefore because of that High Court full-bench judgment that all the changes made to introduce Tamil as both a language subject and the medium of instruction from Standards I to V became inoperative, and the old curriculum continued. That was the position under DMK rule. Do you know what happened under AIADMK rule?
Do you know what Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said in her Independence Day address after raising the flag at Fort St George in 2003?

She first said: “My government has already issued an order to teach the subject called Scientific Tamil progressively from Standards I to V in every category of school, beginning with the 2003-2004 academic year.” But, contradicting this, at the same event Jayalalithaa declared: “On this Independence Day, I am very happy to announce that I have issued an order to teach the subject called Scientific Tamil to every student from LKG to Standard XII, in every category of school in Tamil Nadu, beginning this very year, 2003-2004.”

Having first said that it would be done progressively, and having issued a government order accordingly, the same Chief Minister then said: “I have issued an order to teach Scientific Tamil to every student from LKG to Standard XII in this very academic year.”

From the moment the AIADMK government took office, it has continued to ruin the school education system. No sooner had it arrived than it arbitrarily decided that it would not accept the Uniform System of School Education Act. Although textbooks had already been prepared at a cost of several crores of rupees, it ordered that they not be distributed. Using its majority, it passed an amendment against the Uniform Education Act, announced in great haste tenders for printing new textbooks, and left students without anything to study for three months. It thereby damaged their education. Sixty of the students’ total two hundred working days were wasted.

The Supreme Court ruled that the Uniform System of School Education had to be implemented from Standards I to X. Angered but left with no alternative, the AIADMK government accepted it grudgingly and continued to show complete indifference to students’ education. The Chief Minister herself spoke in the Assembly as though the Supreme Court had committed some wrong. The judges had said that any errors in the Uniform Education textbooks could be removed. Instead of correcting errors, the government, driven by spite, tore out, struck through and concealed everything it disliked.

The law enacted under the DMK government sought to remove the fundamental contradiction by which students could study in Tamil Nadu and graduate from university without ever learning Tamil. By adding one standard each year under that law, we have now reached the point where Tamil is taught as a subject in every school up to Standard X.

Now someone has approached the court seeking a stay of that very requirement, and notice has been issued directing the government to reply by 10 June. This Jayalalithaa government has shelved and failed to implement many schemes introduced and completed by the DMK government. What reply will it give on this vital policy of teaching Tamil as a language subject?

It must not repeat the position it took on the Uniform Education policy. It must not act in the contrary direction of introducing English-medium instruction in primary schools. It must take a sound decision, with genuine concern for Tamil-language education, and place it before the court.
This is the wish of the Tamil people! Will even this wish of the Tamil people be fulfilled?
With affection,
M.K.
